seoTitle: How to Add Someone to an Email Thread in Outlook
metaDescription: Loop a new person into an ongoing Outlook email thread using Reply All, the CC field, or inline @mentions, with context so they're not lost.
intro: Bringing a new teammate into an existing conversation shouldn't mean copying and pasting old messages into a fresh email. Outlook lets you add someone directly to a live thread through Reply All, the CC field, or by typing their name with an @mention, keeping the full history intact for them to review.

steps.heading: Open the thread you want to update
steps.body: Find the email conversation in your inbox that you want the new person added to.

steps.heading: Click Reply All
steps.body: Use Reply All to keep the existing group and context intact rather than starting a new message.

steps.heading: Add the new recipient
steps.body: Click into the To or CC field and type the new person's email address to include them going forward.

steps.heading: Try the @mention shortcut
steps.body: Alternatively, type @ followed by their name directly in the message body, which will often auto-populate the recipient field for you.

steps.heading: Add context and send
steps.body: Write a brief summary line to bring the new person up to speed, then send and check your outbox to confirm they're now part of the thread.

faq.question: Will the new person see the entire email history?
faq.answer: Yes, as long as you use Reply All rather than starting a new email, the full quoted history stays attached below your message.

faq.question: What does typing @ before someone's name do?
faq.answer: It's a shortcut that lets you mention them directly in the message body, which Outlook will often use to automatically add them as a recipient.

faq.question: Should I add someone to To or CC?
faq.answer: Use To if you need a response from them, or CC if you just want them kept in the loop without expecting action.

faq.question: How do I make sure the added person understands the context quickly?
faq.answer: Write a short intro line summarizing where the conversation stands before or after adding them, so they don't have to read the entire history to catch up.
